summary_sections.title: Lesson Overview
summary_sections.content_html:
Overview

This lesson covers the fundamental measures of center used to analyze and summarize data sets. You'll learn how to calculate and apply the mean, median, and mode, and understand when each is most appropriate.

summary_sections.title: Definitions and Concepts
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Definitions

What is the role of p53 in the cell cycle?

p53 is called the 'guardian of the genome' because it responds to DNA damage by activating repair pathways or triggering apoptosis. When DNA damage is detected, p53 activates p21, which inhibits CDK-cyclin complexes and halts the cell cycle at the G₁ checkpoint. If the damage cannot be repaired, p53 activates pro-apoptotic genes (like BAX), causing the cell to self-destruct. About 50% of all human cancers have mutated p53.

What is the difference between a proto-oncogene and an oncogene?

Proto-oncogenes are normal, essential genes that encode proteins promoting cell growth, division, and survival (like growth factor receptors, signal transduction proteins, and transcription factors). They are the 'accelerator' of the cell cycle. When a proto-oncogene is mutated (by point mutation, gene amplification, or chromosomal translocation), it becomes an oncogene — a permanently activated version that drives continuous cell proliferation regardless of normal signals. Examples include RAS (signal transduction) and HER2 (growth factor receptor).

What is apoptosis, and why is it important for preventing cancer?

Apoptosis is programmed cell death — a highly regulated process where the cell shrinks, its DNA is fragmented by enzymes (caspases), and it is neatly packaged into 'apoptotic bodies' that are absorbed by neighboring cells or macrophages without causing inflammation. It is essential for preventing cancer because cells with irreparable DNA damage can self-destruct before they accumulate the mutations needed for tumor formation. Cancer cells often evade apoptosis through mutations in p53 or overexpression of anti-apoptotic proteins like Bcl-2.

What is the difference between a benign tumor and a malignant tumor?

Benign tumors are abnormal growths that remain encapsulated, grow slowly, and do not invade surrounding tissue or spread to distant sites. They are generally not life-threatening (though they can cause problems by pressing on nearby structures). Malignant tumors (cancers) are invasive — they break through tissue boundaries, stimulate blood vessel growth (angiogenesis), and can metastasize by releasing cells into the blood or lymphatic system that establish secondary tumors in distant organs.

What is metastasis?

Metastasis is the process by which cancer cells break away from the primary tumor, enter the bloodstream or lymphatic system, travel to distant organs, and establish new (secondary) tumors. This is what makes cancer most dangerous and difficult to treat. Metastatic cells must acquire several capabilities: detachment from the primary tumor, invasion through the basement membrane, survival in circulation, and colonization of new tissue. Common metastatic sites include lungs, liver, brain, and bones.

summary_sections.title: Outliers and Resistant Measures
summary_sections.content_html:
Outliers and Resistant Measures

Li-Fraumeni syndrome illustrates Knudson's two-hit hypothesis for tumor suppressors. Normally, both copies of p53 must be inactivated for function to be lost. Patients with LFS inherit one defective copy in every cell, so only one somatic mutation (the 'second hit') in any cell is needed to eliminate p53 entirely. Since p53 is the 'guardian of the genome' active in all cell types, its loss removes the key checkpoint that detects DNA damage and triggers apoptosis. This explains why LFS patients develop diverse cancer types at young ages — any tissue can be affected.

Traditional chemotherapy drugs (like cisplatin, doxorubicin, and taxanes) target cells that divide rapidly — which includes cancer cells but also normal cells with high turnover rates. Hair follicle cells (divide every 23-72 hours → hair loss/alopecia), intestinal epithelium (replaced every 3-5 days → nausea/diarrhea), bone marrow stem cells (continuous blood cell production → anemia, low WBCs, low platelets), and oral mucosa (fast turnover → mouth sores). These side effects are the fundamental limitation of non-targeted chemotherapy and the reason targeted therapies were developed.

summary_sections.title: Real-World Applications
summary_sections.content_html:
Real-World Applications

Example: A family has a history where multiple members develop different types of cancer (breast, brain, leukemia, bone) at unusually young ages. Genetic testing reveals that affected members carry a germline mutation in one copy of the TP53 gene (Li-Fraumeni syndrome). Why does losing one copy of p53 dramatically increase cancer risk across many tissue types?

Li-Fraumeni syndrome illustrates Knudson's two-hit hypothesis for tumor suppressors. Normally, both copies of p53 must be inactivated for function to be lost. Patients with LFS inherit one defective copy in every cell, so only one somatic mutation (the 'second hit') in any cell is needed to eliminate p53 entirely. Since p53 is the 'guardian of the genome' active in all cell types, its loss removes the key checkpoint that detects DNA damage and triggers apoptosis. This explains why LFS patients develop diverse cancer types at young ages — any tissue can be affected.

Example: A pathologist examines two tissue biopsy slides. Slide A shows a well-defined mass with cells that closely resemble normal tissue, surrounded by a fibrous capsule, with rare mitotic figures. Slide B shows irregular borders, cells with large abnormal nuclei, many mitotic figures, and cells invading through the basement membrane. How should the pathologist classify each sample?

Pathologists distinguish benign from malignant tumors based on several criteria: (1) Differentiation — benign cells resemble normal tissue (well-differentiated), while malignant cells look abnormal (poorly differentiated/anaplastic). (2) Growth pattern — benign tumors are encapsulated with smooth borders; malignant tumors have irregular, invasive borders. (3) Mitotic index — benign tumors have few dividing cells; malignant tumors show many abnormal mitoses. (4) Invasion — the hallmark of malignancy is invasion through the basement membrane into surrounding tissue.

Example: A patient with chronic myelogenous leukemia (CML) has the Philadelphia chromosome — a translocation between chromosomes 9 and 22 that creates the BCR-ABL fusion oncogene. The oncologist prescribes imatinib (Gleevec), which specifically inhibits the BCR-ABL tyrosine kinase. Within months, the patient's white blood cell count returns to normal. What makes this a landmark example of targeted cancer therapy?

Imatinib (Gleevec, approved 2001) is a paradigm-shifting drug in cancer treatment. The Philadelphia chromosome's BCR-ABL fusion produces a constitutively active tyrosine kinase that continuously signals cell proliferation. Imatinib was rationally designed to fit into the ATP-binding site of BCR-ABL, blocking its kinase activity and shutting down the proliferative signal. CML patients on imatinib achieve ~95% remission rates. This success demonstrated that understanding the specific molecular driver of a cancer can lead to highly effective, targeted therapies with fewer side effects than broad chemotherapy.
